1: kalamos homoios rhabdō — "a measuring rod like a staff." The combination of kalamos (reed, the measurement tool) and rhabdos (staff, the shepherd's or authority-rod) merges two instruments. Ezek 40:3 uses a measuring rod (of six cubits); Zech 2:1-5 uses a measuring line for Jerusalem. In both prophetic texts, measuring signals divine care and preservation — the measured thing belongs to God. metron ton naon tou Theou — "measure the temple of God." The imperative commission: measurement as an act of claiming divine ownership over what is measured.
2: tēn aulēn tēn exōthen ... ekbale exōthen — "the court outside... throw it outside." The outer court is excluded from measurement: it is "given to the nations," and they will trample the holy city for mēnas tesserakonta [kai] dyo (42 months). The 42-month period (= 3.5 years = 1260 days = "time, times, and half a time" of Dan 7:25; 12:7) is the symbolic half-seven — the incomplete time of the nations' domination, bounded and limited. Patēsousin (will trample) echoes Lk 21:24 ("Jerusalem will be trampled on by the Gentiles until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled").
3: tois dysin martyrsin mou — "my two witnesses." The "my" (mou) marks them as belonging to the Lord: they are his witnesses, as Christ is the faithful witness (ho martys ho pistos, 1:5). sakkous (sackcloth) — the mourning garment of the prophets (Isa 20:2; Zech 13:4) signals that the witnesses prophesy in the mode of lamentation, not triumph. 1260 days of sackcloth-prophecy — the prophetic office in the period of the nations' trampling is marked by grief.
4: hai dyo elaiai kai hai dyo lychniai — "the two olive trees and the two lampstands." Zech 4:3,11,14: the two olive trees flanking the golden lampstand are "the two anointed ones who stand before the Lord of the whole earth" — in Zechariah's context, Zerubbabel (civil) and Joshua (priestly). In Revelation the two witnesses together embody both offices. The lampstand connection links them to the seven churches of chs 1-3 (which are lampstands, 1:20): the witnesses are the prophetic-witness function of the church.
5: pyr ekporeuetai ek tou stomatos autōn — "fire comes out of their mouths and devours their enemies." The fire-from-mouth is a prophetic power drawn from Elijah (2 Kgs 1:10-12: Elijah calling fire from heaven) and from Jer 5:14 (YHWH's words in the prophet's mouth as fire that devours). The fire is the fire of the word: the prophetic proclamation that executes judgment. apoktanthēnai autous — "must die in this way" (aorist passive infinitive of necessity); the judgment modality is fixed.
6: kleiein ton ouranon hina mē hyetos brēchē — "to shut the sky so that no rain falls." Explicitly Elijah's power (1 Kgs 17:1; Lk 4:25; Jas 5:17 — Elijah prayed that it would not rain for 3.5 years). strephousin ta hydata eis haima — Moses's power over water-to-blood (Exod 7:19-20). The two witnesses together embody the Elijah-Moses typology: the two great prophets of Israel whose ministries bracket the OT prophetic tradition. Their pairing at the Transfiguration (Matt 17:3) gives this identification christological precedent.
7: to thērion to anabainon ek tēs abyssou — "the beast that rises from the Abyss." This is the beast's first appearance in Revelation (it will be developed in chs 13, 17); the brevity here ("the beast") implies the audience already knows the referent, or that this proleptic mention sets up the fuller narrative. poiēsei meta autōn polemon kai nikēsei autous — "will make war against them and conquer them and kill them." The beast's temporary victory over the witnesses is stated with three aorist future forms (will make, will conquer, will kill) — decisive and brutal.
8: Sodoma kai Aigyptos — "Sodom and Egypt." The great city is identified figuratively (pneumatikōs, spiritually/allegorically) with both Sodom (the city of sexual immorality and wickedness, Gen 18-19) and Egypt (the house of slavery, Exod). The identification "where their Lord was crucified" unmistakably identifies the city as Jerusalem — yet its naming as Sodom and Egypt denies it the special status it might claim. Jerusalem has become what it condemned: the city of God's witnesses is now the city of anti-God empire.
9: blepousin to ptōma autōn hēmeras treis kai hēmisy — "they stare at their bodies for three and a half days." The 3.5-day period mirrors the 3.5-year (42-month/1260-day) ministry period: the same half-seven structure. ou aphiousin ta ptōmata autōn tethēnai eis mnēma — "they do not permit their bodies to be placed in a tomb." Refusing burial was the ultimate dishonor in Jewish culture (Deut 28:26; Ps 79:3; Jer 8:2) and in many ancient cultures — the ultimate humiliation of one's enemies after death.
10: euphrainontai ep' autois kai euphrosunēn poiousin — "they celebrate and exchange gifts." The gift-exchange (pempousin dōra allēlois) on the occasion of the prophets' deaths reads like a dark parody of a festival celebration — comparable to the Purim gift-exchange (Esth 9:22: "days of feasting and joy and giving presents to each other"). The world-system celebrates the silencing of the prophetic witness.
11: pneuma zōēs ek tou Theou eisēlthen en autois — "the breath/spirit of life from God entered them." The language is deliberately Gen 2:7 (God breathed the nishmat chayyim, the breath of life, into Adam) and Ezek 37:10 (the breath entered the dry bones and they stood on their feet — estēsan epi tous podas autōn). Both OT texts are cited almost verbatim: the resurrection of the witnesses is narrated in the vocabulary of creation and covenant-restoration. estēsan epi tous podas autōn — they stood on their feet, the exact phrase of Ezek 37:10.
12: anabate hōde — "Come up here!" — the same words spoken to John in 4:1 (his heavenly ascent to see the throne). The witnesses' ascension in a cloud (en tē nephelē) echoes both the Elijah ascent (2 Kgs 2:11: Elijah taken up in a whirlwind/chariot of fire) and the Christ-ascension (Acts 1:9: "he was taken up before their very eyes, and a cloud hid him from their sight"). etheōrēsan autous hoi echthroi autōn — their enemies watched: the public vindication mirrors the public humiliation of their death.
13: seismos megas egeneto — "a great earthquake occurred." Seismos (earthquake) in Revelation is always associated with divine judgment and theophanic activity (6:12; 8:5; 11:13,19; 16:18). to dekaton tēs poleōs epesen — "a tenth of the city collapsed." The specificity of "a tenth" is unusual in Revelation's symbolic arithmetic (normally thirds and quarters); some see it as reflecting the earthquake's partial scope — unlike the total judgment of the bowls. edothē doxa tō Theō tou ouranou — "they gave glory to the God of heaven" — rare in Revelation (contrast with 16:9,11 where they refuse to repent and give glory); this suggests genuine awe or partial repentance.
14: The transition marker "the second woe has passed; the third woe is coming soon (tachos)" closes the interlude of the two witnesses and the sixth-trumpet complex. Tachos (quickly/soon) maintains the sense of urgency that runs through the book (1:1; 22:6,7,12,20). The structural function of v14 is bridging: the woe-announcement of 9:12 is fulfilled, and the seventh trumpet (the third woe) is introduced.
15: egenonto hai basileiai tou kosmou tou Kyriou hēmōn kai tou Christou autou — "the kingdom of the world has become (aorist: egenonto) the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ." The aorist marks this as a completed transfer — not "is becoming" but "has become." The seventh trumpet sounds, and heaven announces the fait accompli of the eschatological kingdom. kai basileusei eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn — "he will reign forever and ever" (singular: the King, singular, reigns — though "our Lord and his Christ" are two).
16: epesan epi ta prosōpa autōn kai prosekunēsan tō Theō — the twenty-four elders prostrate themselves (piptō + prosekunēsan: fall + worship) exactly as in 4:10 and 5:14; the gesture of prostrate worship frames the throne-room scenes. The verb proskuneō in Revelation appears 24 times — the entire book can be read as a contest between whose worship is legitimate.
17: eilēphas tēn dynamin sou tēn megalēn kai ebasileusas — "you have taken your great power and have begun to reign." Ebasileusas (aorist: you have begun to reign/you have reigned) — the aorist of divine reign is the announcement of the accomplished fact from the heavenly perspective. ho ōn kai ho ēn — "who is and who was." Significantly the third element "who is to come" (ho erchomenos, present in 1:4,8; 4:8) is absent here — because he has now come: the coming is fulfilled.
18: hōrgisthēsan ta ethnē kai ēlthen hē orgē sou — "the nations raged and your wrath came." The verb orgizō is applied first to the nations' rage, then to God's wrath — the same root, mirroring each other: the nations' anger is met by divine anger. The verse condenses Ps 2:1,5 (why do the nations rage... he rebukes them in his wrath) into a single announcement. tous mikrous kai tous megalous — "both small and great" — the leveling formula: divine judgment/reward respects no hierarchy.
19: hē kibōtos tēs diathēkēs autou — "the ark of his covenant." The ark, which disappeared from the narrative of Israel's history after the Babylonian destruction of the Temple (its fate is unknown; 2 Macc 2:4-8 says Jeremiah hid it on Nebo; it was never recovered), is now revealed in the heavenly temple. The unveiling of the heavenly ark at the completion of the seventh trumpet announces the return of the fullness of covenant-presence: what Israel lost is now visible in its eternal, heavenly reality. The Sinai-theophany formula (lightning, thunder, earthquake, hail) frames the revelation as a new Sinai.
